---
title: "Untitled"
format: html
editor: visual
---

## Quarto

Quarto enables you to weave together content and executable code into a finished document. To learn more about Quarto see <https://quarto.org>.

## Running Code

When you click the **Render** button a document will be generated that includes both content and the output of embedded code. You can embed code like this:

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(bayesrules)
library(lubridate)
library(ggplot2)
```

```{r}

df <- read.csv("../data/chicago_vehicle_theft_crimes_2026.csv")
```

You can add options to executable code like this

```{r}
# mdy_hms handles the string formatting "mm/dd/yyyy hh:mm:ss AM" automatically
df$Date <- mdy_hms(df$Date)
# Convert the date to a numeric date since the start of the dataset
df$day_numeric <- as.numeric(difftime(df$Date, min(df$Date), units = "days"))
# Convert the numeric dates to discrete numbers 
# Remove the incomplete current day
df <- df %>% mutate(day_n = floor(day_numeric)) %>% filter(day_n != 52)
```

The `echo: false` option disables the printing of code (only output is displayed).

```{r}
# Aggregate the counts of reported theft cases
daily_counts <- df %>% group_by(day_n) %>% summarize(theft_count = n())
# Plot the counts over days to looks for correlation
ggplot(daily_counts, aes(x = day_n, y = theft_count)) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue") +  # Line shows the trend
  geom_point(color = "darkblue") +  # Points show individual days
  labs(title = "Daily Frequency of Vehicle Thefts",
       x = "Day Number",
       y = "Count of Thefts") +
  theme_minimal()
```

Generally from the plot there is no specific trend from day-to-day which makes using an independence assumption more plausible.

```{r}
# Treating all days as independent events we can construct a density plot
ggplot(daily_counts, aes(x = theft_count)) +
  geom_histogram(fill = "steelblue", color = "black", binwidth = 3) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Daily Theft Counts",
       x = "Number of Thefts per Day",
       y = "Density") +
  theme_minimal()
```

From research into priors the annual car theft rate in Chicago is 8.25 for every 1000 people. Given that Chicago has a population of around 2.72 million, that leads to 22440 total stolen cars per year in Chicago!

Since we are using the independent and identically dsitributed assumption for the car theft rate in Chicago, we expect that the expected rate should be close to 1/365.25 of the annual rate, which leads to a rate of 61.44.

For the variance of the rate, the monthly 5 year counts range from 630 to 3186 which translates into an average daily rate range of 21 to 106 and since each month in a 5 year range accounts for 1 60th of the data, I suggest that choosing a distribution that has tail probabilities around 0.0166. Since the gamma distribution is right skewed I will adjust my prior to have the 0.98 quantile around 106.

$$
\text{Modelling the daily car theft rate as } \lambda \text{ as being distributed as a Gamma distribution: }
$$

$$\lambda \sim Gamma(\alpha, \beta)$$ $$\text{From our assumption that the average rate should be 61.44: }$$
$$\text{E}(\lambda) = \frac{\alpha}{\beta}= 61.44 $$

$$\beta= \frac{\alpha}{61.44}$$

$$
\text{From the assumption about the 0.98th quantile being 106:}
$$

$$
\text{P}(\lambda \le 106) = 0.98
$$

$$
\text{Substituting the CDF of a Gamma distribution:}
$$

$$
\text{P}(\lambda \le x) = F(x; \alpha, \beta) =\int_{0}^{x} \frac{\beta^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)} t^{\alpha-1} e^{-\beta t} \, dt =   \frac{\gamma(\alpha, \beta x)}{\Gamma(\alpha)}
$$

$$ \text{Substituting the contraint from the expectation of a Gamma distribution:} $$

$$ \text{P}(\lambda \le 106) =\frac{\gamma(\alpha, \frac{106}{61.44}\alpha)}{\Gamma(\alpha)} = 0.98$$

$$ \text{Unfortunately analytically solving this is difficult so to computationally solve the unique solution for }\alpha :$$

$$ F(106; \alpha,\frac{\alpha}{61.44}) = 0.98$$

$$  F^{-1}(0.98; \alpha,\frac{\alpha}{61.44})= \text{qgamma}(0.98, \alpha,\frac{\alpha}{61.44}) = 106 $$

$$
\text{Solving the single root of the funtion } g(\alpha):
$$

$$  g(\alpha) = \text{qgamma}(0.98, \alpha,\frac{\alpha}{61.44}) - 106 = 0 $$

```{r}
# Targets for gamma
target_mean <- 61.44
target_q98 <- 106

# 2. Define a function that finds the difference between 
# the actual 98th quantile and our target
find_alpha <- function(alpha) {
  # Calculate beta based on the current alpha and known mean
  beta <- alpha / target_mean
  
  # Calculate what the 98th quantile is with this alpha/beta
  current_q98 <- qgamma(0.98, shape = alpha, rate = beta)
  
  # Return the difference (we want this to be zero)
  return(current_q98 - target_q98)
}

# 3. Use uniroot to solve for alpha
# We search for alpha between a small value (0.1) and a large value (100)
solution <- uniroot(find_alpha, lower = 0.01, upper = 100)

prior_alpha <- solution$root
prior_beta <- final_alpha / target_mean

print(paste("Shape (Alpha):", round(final_alpha, 4)))
print(paste("Rate (Beta):", round(final_beta, 4)))
```

```{r}
plot_gamma(prior_alpha, prior_beta)
```

Using a gamma-poisson model has conjugate priors:

```{r}
total = sum(daily_counts$theft_count)
days = length(daily_counts$day_n)
plot_gamma_poisson(6.144,0.1, total, days)
```

resulting in the following posterior:

```{r}
alpha <- 6.144+total
beta <- 0.1 + days
plot_gamma(alpha, beta)
```

```{r}
summarize_gamma_poisson(6.144,0.1, total, days)
```

Using this we can construct a 95% credible interval for the mean rate for tomorrows car theft rate

```{r}
qgamma(c(0.025,0.975), alpha, beta)
```

We can also simulate the actual number stolen tomorrow and construct the predictive interval

```{r}
set.seed(84735)
# Simulate 500,000 potential average theft rates
n_sims <- 500000
lambda_sims <- rgamma(n_sims, shape = alpha, rate = beta)

# For each lambda, simulate a number of thefts
predictive_thefts <- rpois(n_sims, lambda = lambda_sims)
quantile(predictive_thefts, c(0.025, 0.975))

```

```{r}
predictive_df <- data.frame(thefts = predictive_thefts)

ggplot(predictive_df, aes(x = thefts)) +
  geom_bar(aes(y = after_stat(count / sum(count))), 
           fill = "forestgreen", 
           color = "black") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = mean(predictive_df$thefts), 
             color = "red", 
             linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "Posterior Predictive Distribution of Daily Thefts",
       subtitle = "Red dashed line represents the mean predicted daily count",
       x = "Predicted Number of Thefts",
       y = "Probability") +
  theme_minimal()
```
